01_initial_request.target_name: BRCA1 and BRCA2 Variants of Uncertain Significance (VUS)
01_initial_request.input_data: Archival BRCA1 and BRCA2 genetic testing data from 3,544 subjects, comprising 236 unique variants identified in 459 patients, along with clinical databases and family history records
01_initial_request.user_intent: To define the clinical significance of VUS through multifactorial analysis to establish consensus management guidelines and improve patient risk stratification
01_initial_request.quantifiable_goal: Reclassification of VUS into definitive clinical significance categories (pathogenic/likely pathogenic or benign/likely benign)

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] A cohort of 3,544 patients with personal cancer or breast cancer risk was recruited for BRCA1/BRCA2 genetic testing. [Gap] Genomic DNA needed to be sequenced to identify variants. [Decision] To achieve variant identification, chose to use MiSeq or NextSeq (Illumina) to perform genomic DNA sequencing on patient blood samples, expecting to obtain paired sequencing reads.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Paired sequencing reads generated from 3,544 subjects

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Sequencing reads were generated from patient samples. [Gap] Reads needed to be aligned to reference genome for variant calling. [Decision] To achieve variant identification, chose to use reference genome mapping to perform read alignment on GRCh37/hg19, expecting to obtain mapped sequencing reads.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Paired sequencing reads mapped to human reference genome sequence

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Sequencing reads were mapped to reference genome. [Gap] Variants needed to be annotated for functional interpretation. [Decision] To achieve variant annotation, chose to use Ensembl Variant Effect Predictor to perform variant annotation on called variants, expecting to obtain annotated variant effects.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Variants annotated with functional effects

references.citation: McLaren W, et al. The ensembl variant effect predictor. Genome Biol. 2016;17(1):122.
references.relevance: Provides the variant annotation tool used in this study

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Variants were annotated from sequencing data. [Gap] Common polymorphisms needed to be filtered out to focus on rare variants. [Decision] To achieve variant filtering, chose to use 1000 Genomes Projects database to perform MAF filtering on variants, expecting to exclude common variants.

parameters.filtering_criteria: Variants with MAF ≥1% excluded from manual curation

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Variants with MAF ≥1% excluded from analysis

references.citation: 1000 Genomes Project Consortium, et al. A global reference for human genetic variation. Nature. 2015;526(7571):68–74.
references.relevance: Provides population frequency data for variant filtering

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Variants were filtered based on population frequency. [Gap] Variants needed to be classified according to clinical significance standards. [Decision] To achieve variant classification, chose to use ACMG/AMP guidelines to perform five-tier classification on variants, expecting to obtain pathogenicity classifications.

parameters.classification_system: five-tier classes: pathogenic, likely pathogenic, uncertain significance, likely benign, and benign
parameters.organization: American College of Medical Genetics and Genomics and Association for Molecular Pathology

02_agent_trajectory.observation: 236 unique variants identified in 459 patients (231 VUS and 5 likely benign at initial classification)

references.citation: Richards S, et al. Standards and guidelines for the interpretation of sequence variants. Genet Med. 2015;17(5):405–24.
references.relevance: Provides the classification guidelines used in this study

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Initial variant classifications were assigned. [Gap] Additional evidence from population databases needed to be gathered for reclassification. [Decision] To achieve comprehensive variant review, chose to use Exome Variant Viewer to perform population frequency review on variants, expecting to obtain variant frequency data.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Variant frequency information extracted from exome sequencing data

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Population frequency data was being gathered from multiple sources. [Gap] Additional population frequency data from gnomAD needed for comprehensive review. [Decision] To achieve comprehensive variant review, chose to use Genome Aggregation Database (gnomAD) to perform population frequency review on variants, expecting to obtain variant frequency data across diverse populations.

parameters.data_type: exome or genome sequencing on unselected populations

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Variant frequency information extracted from diverse populations

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Population frequency data was collected. [Gap] Clinical database evidence needed to be reviewed for variant pathogenicity. [Decision] To achieve comprehensive variant review, chose to use ClinVar to perform clinical variant classification review on variants, expecting to obtain expert-reviewed variant classifications.

parameters.data_type: clinical databases with variant classifications proposed by depositor and reviewed by expert panels

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Clinical variant classification information extracted

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Clinical database evidence was being gathered. [Gap] Additional clinical variant data from LOVD needed for comprehensive review. [Decision] To achieve comprehensive variant review, chose to use Leiden Open Variation Database (LOVD) to perform clinical variant review on variants, expecting to obtain combined data from individuals with disease history.

parameters.data_type: combined data from individuals with personal or family history of disease

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Clinical variant information from disease-affected individuals extracted

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Multiple clinical databases were reviewed. [Gap] Integrated database analysis from VarSome needed for comprehensive classification. [Decision] To achieve comprehensive variant review, chose to use VarSome to perform integrated variant classification review on variants, expecting to obtain combined database and in silico predictions.

parameters.data_type: relies on vast quantities of databases and in silico analysis prediction programs

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Integrated variant classification information extracted

references.citation: Kopanos C, et al. VarSome: the human genomic variant search engine. Bioinformatics. 2019;35(11):1978–80.
references.relevance: Provides the integrated variant search engine used in this study

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] General variant databases were reviewed. [Gap] BRCA-specific database evidence needed for gene-specific pathogenicity assessment. [Decision] To achieve comprehensive variant review, chose to use BRCA Exchange to perform BRCA-specific variant review on variants, expecting to obtain worldwide contributed pathogenicity data.

parameters.data_type: integrated database containing information about pathogenicity contributed by worldwide data depositors

02_agent_trajectory.observation: BRCA-specific pathogenicity information extracted

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] BRCA-specific databases were reviewed. [Gap] Protein functional information needed for variant interpretation. [Decision] To achieve comprehensive variant review, chose to use Uniprot to perform protein functional information review on variants, expecting to obtain protein sequence and functional domain information.

parameters.data_type: resource of protein sequences and functional information

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Protein sequence and functional domain information extracted

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Database evidence was collected. [Gap] In silico prediction tools needed to assess variant impact on protein function. [Decision] To achieve variant pathogenicity assessment, chose to use SIFT to perform in silico prediction on variants, expecting to obtain tolerance predictions for amino acid changes.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: In silico prediction results for variant tolerance obtained

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] SIFT predictions were generated. [Gap] Additional in silico prediction from PolyPhen-2 needed for comprehensive assessment. [Decision] To achieve variant pathogenicity assessment, chose to use PolyPhen-2 to perform in silico prediction on variants, expecting to obtain phenotyping predictions for amino acid changes.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: In silico prediction results for variant phenotyping obtained

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] PolyPhen-2 predictions were generated. [Gap] Additional in silico prediction from AlignGVGD needed for comprehensive assessment. [Decision] To achieve variant pathogenicity assessment, chose to use AlignGVGD to perform in silico prediction on variants, expecting to obtain Grantham variation and deviation scores.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: In silico prediction results for Grantham scores obtained

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] In silico predictions were completed. [Gap] Family segregation data needed to assess variant penetrance and disease susceptibility. [Decision] To achieve variant pathogenicity assessment, chose to use Segregation Analysis to perform family pedigree studies on variant carriers, expecting to obtain co-segregation probabilities.

parameters.calculation: probabilities for co-segregation of phenotype and genetic variant by chance

02_agent_trajectory.observation: 8 out of 26 (30.7%) VUS considered for reclassification based on co-segregation studies

references.citation: Møller P, et al. A Simplified method for Segregation Analysis (SISA). Hum Mutat. 2011;32(5):568–71.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Segregation analysis was performed. [Gap] Co-occurrence data needed to assess variant neutrality when present with pathogenic mutations. [Decision] To achieve variant pathogenicity assessment, chose to use Co-Occurrence Analysis to perform trans configuration analysis on variants, expecting to obtain neutrality interpretations.

parameters.analysis_type: co-existence of variant with pathogenic mutation in same gene

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Co-occurrence data assessed for variant neutrality

references.citation: Zuradelli M, et al. Four new cases of double heterozygosity for BRCA1 and BRCA2 gene mutations. Breast Cancer Res Treat. 2010;124(1):251–8.
references.relevance: Provides evidence on double heterozygosity rarity

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Co-occurrence analysis was completed. [Gap] Loss of heterozygosity data from tumor samples needed to distinguish deleterious mutations from benign polymorphisms. [Decision] To achieve variant pathogenicity assessment, chose to use LOH Analysis to perform NGS analysis on somatic tumors from VUS carriers, expecting to obtain mutant allele fraction data.

parameters.analysis_type: NGS analysis on genomic landscapes of somatic tumors
parameters.interpretation: High mutant allele fraction implied loss of heterozygosity

02_agent_trajectory.observation: LOH confirmed in four variants, implying deleterious mutation rather than benign polymorphism

references.citation: Osorio A, et al. Loss of heterozygosity analysis at the BRCA loci in tumor samples. Int J Cancer. 2002;99(2):305–9.
references.relevance: Provides methodology for LOH analysis at BRCA loci

references.citation: Sorscher S, Ramkissoon S. Next-Generation Sequencing to Better Characterize a BRCA Variant. Case Rep Oncol. 2017;10(2):634–7.
references.relevance: Provides evidence on NGS for VUS characterization

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] LOH analysis was performed on tumor samples. [Gap] Pathogenic/likely pathogenic variants needed orthogonal validation. [Decision] To achieve variant validation, chose to use Sanger Sequencing to perform orthogonal validation on variants classified as pathogenic or likely pathogenic, expecting to obtain confirmed variant calls.

parameters.target_variants: Variants from NGS classified/reclassified as pathogenic or likely pathogenic

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Pathogenic/likely pathogenic variants orthogonally validated

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Variant validation was completed. [Gap] Family cancer history needed to be reviewed for both variant carriers and normal controls. [Decision] To achieve comprehensive clinical assessment, chose to use Family History Review to perform medical record and phone clarification on variant carriers and normal controls, expecting to obtain updated cancer history data.

parameters.review_method: medical records or over the phone for clarification
parameters.review_subjects: families on both variant carriers and normal control individuals

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Family cancer history updated for variant carriers and normal controls

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] All evidence was collected from databases, in silico tools, segregation, LOH, and family history. [Gap] Variants needed to be reclassified based on accumulated evidence. [Decision] To achieve VUS reclassification, chose to use Annual Review Process to perform variant reclassification on all VUS and likely benign variants, expecting to obtain updated clinical significance categories.

parameters.evidence_sources: clinical databases, family studies, in silico analysis, LOH, functional studies

02_agent_trajectory.observation: 26 unique variants (11.0%) reclassified; 13 VUS upgraded to pathogenic/likely pathogenic (31 probands, 6.8%); 8 VUS downgraded to likely benign (69 probands, 15%)

03_success_verification.validation_technique: Multifactorial analysis combining clinical databases, segregation analysis, co-occurrence analysis, in silico predictions, LOH analysis, and family history review

total_subjects_tested.interpretation: Total cohort size for BRCA1/BRCA2 genetic testing

unique_variants_identified.interpretation: BRCA1: 86; BRCA2: 150 unique variants identified in 459 patients

reclassification_rate_patients.interpretation: 146/459 patients had variants reclassified during review

upgraded_to_pathogenic.interpretation: 31 probands had VUS reclassified to pathogenic or likely pathogenic, offered high-risk surveillance

downgraded_to_benign.interpretation: 69 probands had VUS downgraded to cancer risk equivalent to general population

mean_reclassification_time.interpretation: Mean time from first report to reclassification (range: 1.3 to 149.4 months)

median_reclassification_time.interpretation: Median time from first report to reclassification

03_success_verification.final_verdict: The research objective was achieved. The study successfully reclassified 26 unique VUS (11.0%) affecting 146 patients (31.8%) into definitive clinical significance categories. 13 variants were upgraded to pathogenic/likely pathogenic (affecting 31 probands, 6.8%) and 8 variants were downgraded to likely benign (affecting 69 probands, 15%), enabling appropriate clinical management changes including high-risk surveillance for upgraded cases and risk reduction for downgraded cases.
